name: HTTPX
what_it_does: Modern Python HTTP client with both sync and async APIs. Drop-in requests replacement with async support, HTTP/2, connection pooling, streaming, and automatic content decoding. First-class async support via AsyncClient makes it the standard HTTP client for async Python agents and FastAPI applications.
use_cases: Make async HTTP calls to LLM APIs and external services in FastAPI/async Python agent backends with AsyncClient
Replace requests with httpx in Python agents for HTTP/2 support and connection pooling improvements
Build Python agents that call multiple APIs concurrently using asyncio.gather() with AsyncClient
Stream LLM API responses in async Python agents using httpx's streaming response support
Write Python integration tests against HTTP APIs using httpx.Client for both sync and async test contexts
not_for: Simple synchronous scripts where requests is already familiar — httpx adds complexity for pure sync use cases
High-volume web scraping — httpx is well-suited but aiohttp has lower overhead for extreme concurrency
gRPC calls — httpx is HTTP/REST only; use grpcio for gRPC communication
best_when: You're building an async Python agent (FastAPI, asyncio) that needs HTTP calls — httpx's AsyncClient is the standard choice for async Python HTTP.
avoid_when: You have a simple synchronous Python script with requests already installed — httpx adds no benefit over requests in purely sync contexts.

auth.notes: HTTP client library — auth headers/tokens set by caller via auth parameter or headers.

pricing.notes: BSD license. Part of the encode organization (Starlette, Uvicorn, Django REST Framework maintainers).

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local HTTP client. Performance limited by network and connection pool size.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: HTTPStatusError includes response body for debugging. ConnectError and TimeoutException are distinct. RequestError hierarchy allows catching all transport errors vs. HTTP errors separately.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: GET requests are idempotent by HTTP spec. POST/PUT require application-level idempotency. httpx does not add idempotency keys.
known_agent_gotchas: AsyncClient must be used as an async context manager (async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:) to ensure connection pool cleanup — creating AsyncClient without context manager leaks connections
response.raise_for_status() raises HTTPStatusError — unlike requests which uses HTTPError; adjust exception handling when migrating from requests
HTTP/2 requires h2 package installed separately (pip install httpx[http2]) — not enabled by default; import fails silently without clear error
Timeout applies to the entire request by default — use httpx.Timeout(connect=5.0, read=30.0) for separate connect/read timeouts for streaming LLM responses
AsyncClient is not thread-safe — use one AsyncClient per async task context or protect with locks in multi-threaded async code
Redirects are followed by default (up to 20) — use follow_redirects=False for APIs that use 3xx semantically; check max_redirects parameter for redirect loop protection

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS verification enabled by default. Certificate verification on by default — disable only for development (verify=False). No ambient credential storage.
